# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, dataset composition, task-format and verification procedures, model performance results, failure-mode analysis, and stated limitations for SWE-Bench Pro.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: SWE-Bench Pro abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-Bench Pro contains 1,865 problems drawn from 41 actively maintained repositories.

## Source Excerpt

Abstract: “SWE-Bench Pro contains 1,865 problems sourced from a diverse set of 41 actively maintained repositories.”

## Note

Dataset-size statement from the abstract.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: SWE-Bench Pro abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The benchmark is partitioned into 11 public repositories, 12 held-out repositories, and 18 proprietary commercial repositories.

## Source Excerpt

Abstract: “The benchmark is partitioned into a public set with open access to problems sourced from 11 repositories, a held-out set of 12 repositories and a commercial set of 18 proprietary repositories.”

## Note

This describes repository partitioning, not instance counts.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: claim-support
citation: Section 3.3 Public set
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The public subset contains 731 released instances.

## Source Excerpt

Section 3.3: “Public. We release 731 instances openly on HuggingFace and report the relevant statistics and model performances in this paper.”

## Note

This is the published public-set instance count.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: claim-support
citation: Section 3.3 Commercial set
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The commercial subset contains 276 problems and is kept private while results are reported publicly.

## Source Excerpt

Section 3.3: “Commercial. For the commercial set of 276 problems sourced from startup repositories, we keep it private but report results publicly in this paper and will update in the leaderboard.”

## Note

This is the published commercial-set instance count and access policy.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Section 3.3 Held-Out set
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The held-out subset contains 858 problems and is kept private for future overfitting checks.

## Source Excerpt

Section 3.3: “Held-Out. We hold out a set of 858 problems mirroring the public set but use a separate set of repositories. We keep this set private to test for overfitting in the future.”

## Note

This is the published held-out-set instance count and intended use.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: claim-support
citation: Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-Bench Pro tasks are described as long-horizon tasks that may take professional software engineers hours to days.

## Source Excerpt

Abstract: “Our benchmark features long-horizon tasks that may require hours to days for a professional software engineer to complete.”

## Note

This is a task-duration characterization stated by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Introduction, second contribution
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Reference solutions average 107.4 lines of code across 4.1 files.

## Source Excerpt

Introduction: “On average, the reference solutions span 107.4 lines of code across 4.1 files.”

## Note

This quantifies typical patch size in the benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Introduction, second contribution
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Every SWE-Bench Pro problem requires at least 10 lines of change, and more than 100 tasks require over 100 lines of modification.

## Source Excerpt

Introduction: “Every problem involves at least 10 lines of change, and over 100 tasks demand more than 100 lines of modification.”

## Note

This states minimum and upper-tail edit sizes.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: Section 3.2 Environments
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each task is evaluated in a containerized, language-specific environment, and the environments are to be released as pre-built Docker images.

## Source Excerpt

Section 3.2: “Each task is evaluated in a containerized, language-specific environment with full dependency resolution... All environments will be released as pre-built docker images to ensure that they are fully reproducible.”

## Note

This describes the evaluation environment setup.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Section 3.2 Tests
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The benchmark runs each test set three times and filters out tests that do not pass consistently to reduce dynamic or occasional test failures.

## Source Excerpt

Section 3.2: “We notice that some tests can be dynamic or fail occasionally. To mitigate it, we run each set of tests 3 times and filter out any test that doesn’t pass consistently.”

## Note

This evidence matters because it documents instability in raw tests and the filtering step used to bound flakiness.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Table 1 public-set performance
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On the public set, GPT-5 achieved the highest reported resolve rate at 23.3%.

## Source Excerpt

Table 1: “OpenAI GPT-5 23.3” and Abstract: “GPT-5 achieving the highest score to date at 23.3%.”

## Note

This is the top reported public-set Pass@1 result in the paper.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Table 2 commercial-set performance
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On the commercial set, Claude Opus 4.1 achieved the highest reported resolve rate at 17.8%.

## Source Excerpt

Table 2: “Claude Opus 4.1 17.8.”

## Note

This is the top reported commercial-set resolve rate in the paper.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Introduction performance summary
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The best reported commercial-set score is lower than the best reported public-set score, indicating a public-to-commercial performance gap in this benchmark.

## Source Excerpt

Introduction: “We additionally observe a marked performance gap between the public and commercial sets” and “≤ 23.3% on the public set; ≤ 17.8% on the commercial set.”

## Note

The paper presents this as evidence that commercial enterprise codebases are more difficult.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: result
citation: Section 6.2 failure modes, Opus 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For Claude Opus 4.1 failures, wrong solutions accounted for 35.9% and syntax errors for 24.2%.

## Source Excerpt

Section 6.2: “Opus 4.1 primarily fails on semantic understanding, with wrong solutions accounting for 35.9% of failures and syntax errors at 24.2%.”

## Note

This is part of the paper’s failure-bucket analysis.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: result
citation: Section 6.2 failure modes, Sonnet 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For Claude Sonnet 4 failures, context overflow accounted for 35.6% and endless file reading for 17.0%.

## Source Excerpt

Section 6.2: “Sonnet 4 has context overflow as its primary failure mode (35.6%) and substantial endless file reading behaviors (17.0%).”

## Note

This is another model-specific failure distribution reported by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-015
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: incident
citation: Section 7.1 Limitations
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16941
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper states that language coverage is limited and that Java, C++, and Rust are underrepresented.

## Source Excerpt

Section 7.1: “Although SWE-Bench Pro includes multiple programming languages (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go), the distribution is not uniform, and some widely-used languages like Java, C++, and Rust are underrepresented.”

## Note

This is an explicit benchmark limitation that bounds claims about broad language coverage.
---
